main_summary.summary: Oral history interview with Dorothy Foreman Cotton, conducted July 25, 2011 in Ithaca, New York, by Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill for the Civil Rights History Project. Cotton recounts her childhood in Goldsboro, North Carolina; her mother's death when Cotton was three; her relationship with English teacher Rosa Gray, who arranged for Cotton's admission to Shaw University; her transfer with Dr. Robert Prentiss Daniel to Virginia State College; her marriage to George Cotton; and her work in Petersburg with Reverend Wyatt Tee Walker at Gillfield Baptist Church and the Petersburg Improvement Association. She describes traveling with Walker and James R. Wood to join SCLC in Atlanta in 1960 and becoming Director of the SCLC Citizenship Education Program. She narrates the program's lineage from Esau Jenkins, Septima Clark, Myles Horton, and Highlander Folk School; the relocation of the workshops to Dorchester Academy with Marshall Field Foundation funding; her recruiting and teaching alongside Andrew Young and Septima Clark; her close working relationship with Dr. King; the assassination's aftermath under Ralph Abernathy's leadership; her transition to Head Start director in Jefferson County, Alabama; and the founding of the Dorothy Cotton Institute at Cornell.
main_summary.historical_significance:
Cotton was the only woman in SCLC senior staff meetings during the organization's peak years and ran the program (the Citizenship Education Program) through which an estimated five to eight thousand local Black Southerners passed in five-day residential workshops at Dorchester Academy. Her account documents the institutional transfer of the program from Highlander Folk School to SCLC, the recruiting logic explained to her by Albert Minnis, the pedagogical method she developed (Socratic dialogue rather than lecture, illustrated by the woman who learned to ask why she could not be the city clerk), and the program's connection to voter registration and the broader voting-rights struggle.

The interview also provides Cotton's first-person testimony on Dr. King in private settings — at the parsonage dinner in Petersburg, in her Atlanta apartment as he weighed the Vietnam speech, at his kitchen table debating Stokely Carmichael, on the plane to Oslo with Alberta Williams King, in Jamaica when J. Edgar Hoover called him the most notorious liar — and on the disorientation of SCLC under Ralph Abernathy after the assassination. Cotton's framing of segregation as 'American-style apartheid' and her insistence that the Movement 'did something other than march' anchor a corrective to leader-centric Movement narratives.

chapters.title: Goldsboro Childhood and Father
chapters.summary: Cotton describes 917 North Greenleaf Street in Goldsboro, North Carolina, where she was born; her mother Maggie's death when Cotton was three; her father Claude Foreman raising four girls; his third-grade education and work at a Goldsboro tobacco factory; and her mixed feelings about his strap discipline, traced through her aunt Penny's account of her father's own upbringing. Cotton recounts her father's later move to Buffalo, New York, where he worked at Bethlehem Steel, and his quiet pride at the airport.
related_events: Cotton family relocation to Buffalo NY

chapters.title: Miss Rosa Gray and Shaw University
chapters.summary: Cotton recalls her Goldsboro high school English teacher Rosa Gray, who cast her in lead roles in the annual play, praised her poetry recitation ('there's your ready girl'), and pleaded with Cotton's father on the porch to let her attend the junior-senior prom. Gray then telephoned Shaw University and secured Cotton's admission plus two jobs — in the dining hall and cleaning the teachers' dormitory — to fund her schooling.

chapters.title: Daniel Household and Marriage to George Cotton
chapters.summary: Cotton describes becoming housekeeper at the Shaw University president's residence under Dr. Robert Prentiss Daniel and his wife Blanche, the role she retained when the Daniels moved to Virginia State College in Petersburg. She recounts taking Shakespeare alongside classmate Harris Sims, meeting George Cotton through Sims, and George's formal request to Dr. Daniel for her hand at the foot of the president's staircase.
related_events: Daniel's move from Shaw to Virginia State College in 1950

chapters.title: Petersburg Improvement Association
chapters.summary: Cotton joins Gillfield Baptist Church under Reverend Wyatt Tee Walker, who heads the regional NAACP and the Petersburg Improvement Association. She becomes secretary of the NAACP chapter and trains youth to picket Petersburg's segregated public library and the Woolworth's downtown. Walker invites Reverend Martin Luther King Jr. from Montgomery to address a mass meeting at the church; Cotton recites a poem on the program and meets Dr. King over dinner at the parsonage hosted by the Young Women's Parish Club.

chapters.title: Arriving at SCLC in Atlanta
chapters.summary: After completing her master's degree at Boston University in summer 1960, Cotton tells her husband she will go to Atlanta for three months to help Walker; she stays twenty-three years. She finds three people in the early SCLC office on Auburn Avenue — Lily Hunter, a woman named Brown, and Ms. Ella Baker. She finds housing first with Mrs. Mosley, then briefly with Dora McDonald (who became Dr. King's secretary), then her own apartment. She begins as administrative assistant to Walker.

chapters.title: Highlander, Septima Clark, Esau Jenkins
chapters.summary: Dr. King sends Cotton to Highlander Folk School in the Cumberland Mountains, where she meets Septima Clark and learns the program's origin: Esau Jenkins of Johns Island, South Carolina, used his school-bus route to teach Sea Islands residents about voter registration after a white man shot a Black boy with impunity. Septima Clark — fired from the Charleston school system for refusing to disavow NAACP membership — joined Highlander and worked with Jenkins on cursive-writing literacy training tied to voter registration.

chapters.title: Citizenship Education Program at Dorchester
chapters.summary: When Tennessee confiscates Highlander's property, the Marshall Field Foundation transfers the literacy-and-citizenship training funds to SCLC. Cotton becomes Director of the Citizenship Education Program. Andrew Young secures Dorchester Academy in McIntosh, Georgia, through his Congregational Church connections. Cotton, Young, and Septima Clark travel the South recruiting people from protest hotbeds to five-day residential workshops, where Cotton learns from Albert Minnis (the 'junk man') to lead with the offer of paid travel.
related_events: Transfer of citizenship-education funding from Highlander to SCLC

chapters.title: Workshop Pedagogy and 'If Your Back's Not Bent'
chapters.summary: Cotton describes her Socratic workshop method: rather than lecturing, she drew out attendees' grievances and asked questions that turned victims into agents — illustrated by the woman whose well water the town clerk refused to test, whom Cotton asked, 'Why aren't you the city clerk?' She estimates between five and eight thousand people came through the five-day workshops over the years, recruited through repeat attenders like Fannie Lou Hamer from Ruleville, Mississippi. Dr. King closed one workshop with the line that became Cotton's book title: 'Nobody can ride your back if your back's not bent.'

chapters.title: Gender, SCLC Staff Dynamics, and King's Inner Circle
chapters.summary: Cotton reflects on being the only woman at SCLC executive staff meetings, the coffee and note-taking expectations, and Jack O'Dell's intervention telling her to stay at the table. She describes Dr. King as a listener who let debates run before speaking, naming his 'team of wild horses' — Hosea Williams, C.T. Vivian, James Bevel, and others. She recounts gathering with Dr. King, Bernard Lee, and Andrew Young in Jamaica when J. Edgar Hoover called King 'the most notorious liar in the country,' and the trip to Oslo for the Nobel Prize where Alberta Williams King led singing on the airplane.
related_events: 1964 Nobel Peace Prize trip to Oslo
Hoover's 'most notorious liar' attack on King

chapters.title: Vietnam, Stokely Carmichael, and Nonviolence
chapters.summary: Cotton describes Dr. King coming to her Atlanta apartment, 'giggly like a child,' during the staff debate at Ebenezer Baptist Church about whether he should speak publicly against the Vietnam War. She narrates the SCLC board member Marion Logan signing a letter — Cotton believes Logan did not write it — urging King not to give the speech, and how the criticism strengthened his resolve. She recalls sitting at King's kitchen table with Stokely Carmichael, who quoted Frantz Fanon and argued for abandoning nonviolence; King replied that if he were 'the last lone voice' for nonviolence he would still speak. Cotton later challenged Carmichael at Cornell on his 'Take Up the Gun' refrain by asking him to name who he would shoot.

chapters.title: After the Assassination: Abernathy and Head Start
chapters.summary: Cotton describes the three-year mourning at SCLC after King's assassination, the difficulty of Ralph Abernathy stepping into the presidency over the 'team of wild horses,' and overhearing Abernathy say on the phone, 'I would do it but I don't have anybody to help me.' She praises Abernathy's pastoral care of his Ebenezer Baptist congregation but says he was 'in over his head' leading SCLC. Andrew Young suggested she take an interim Head Start director post in Jefferson County, Alabama, and she moved to Birmingham. She later worked for Maynard Jackson in Atlanta.
related_events: Cotton's Head Start directorship in Jefferson County

chapters.title: Dorothy Cotton Institute and Movement Memory
chapters.summary: Cotton recounts Anke Wessels of Cornell's Center for Transformative Action proposing the Dorothy Cotton Institute, which Cotton accepted as an answer to her question of what would become of her papers and honorary degrees, including one from Ithaca College. She describes the Institute's steering committee meeting in Ithaca and her insistence that it preserve the Movement's 'spiritual quality.' She closes by criticizing the leader-centric distortion of Movement history — that Jesse Jackson and three or four others get lifted up as the Movement — and reaffirms her purpose in writing her book to document the Citizenship Education Program and the thousands who 'did something other than march.'
related_events: Founding of the Dorothy Cotton Institute at Cornell
Cotton's award from the National Civil Rights Museum
